turns.content: I need your help with something that has nothing to do with architecture for once. I have been looking at my grandfather's old letters — he wrote in Arabic, in this beautiful flowing Naskh script — and I want to learn to replicate his handwriting. not just Arabic calligraphy in general but his specific way of forming the letters. is that even possible to teach yourself

It's absolutely possible, though it takes patience. If you have his original letters, you actually have the best possible learning resource — his own hand as your model. Are you already familiar with reading Arabic script, or would you be starting from scratch with the letterforms?

I can read Arabic, yes — not fluently, my reading is slow, like a child sounding out words — but I know the alphabet and the connected forms. my grandparents spoke Arabic at home and I grew up hearing it, but we were never formally taught to write. so I recognize Naskh when I see it, I know what makes it different from Thuluth or Diwani, but my hand has no muscle memory for it

That's a great foundation — knowing the letterforms and being able to identify the script style puts you well ahead. For replicating your grandfather's specific hand, here's what I'd suggest:

1. **Trace first.** Place tracing paper over his letters and follow the strokes slowly. Pay attention to where he lifts the pen, the angle of his downstrokes, how he connects letters. Every writer has personal habits.
2. **Photograph and enlarge.** Scan individual words and blow them up to study the details. Look for consistent quirks — those are his signature.
3. **Practice with a reed pen or bamboo qalam.** Traditional Naskh was written with a cut reed that creates specific thick-thin variations a modern pen can't replicate.
4. **Start with individual letters, then connections.** The flow between letters is where personality shows most in Arabic calligraphy.

the tracing idea is good but I am terrified of damaging the letters. they are old — 1950s, maybe early 1960s — and the paper is fragile, thin as onion skin in some places. I have been keeping them in a box with tissue paper between the sheets

That's a legitimate concern — you definitely don't want to risk the originals. I'd suggest scanning them first and working entirely from digital copies. Scan at 600 DPI minimum in color, even if the ink looks just black — the color scan captures paper tone, foxing, and subtle ink pressure variations. Use a flatbed scanner rather than a phone camera, and handle the letters with clean dry hands or cotton gloves.
would scanning them and then tracing from the printed scans work just as well? I could enlarge specific words and print them at whatever size I need for practice

Exactly — that's the ideal workflow. Scan once, preserve the originals safely, then print at any size you need. You could even isolate individual letterforms in Photoshop or Illustrator and create custom practice sheets — rows of his specific alif, his ba, his way of writing common words. It's like building a font from his handwriting, but for your hand rather than a computer.

I love that idea — a practice sheet made from his own letters. like an alphabet of his hand. there is something poetic about that, no? learning to write from a man who has been gone for thirty years by tracing the ghost of his pen. Darwish wrote something about how the absent are more present than the living. I think about that often

That's a powerful image — an alphabet built from his hand, a way of bringing his presence into your own practice. It's a kind of conversation across time. Once you've learned his letterforms, every time you write in his style you're carrying something of him forward. Do you have access to a scanner, or do you need to find one?

I think the office has a large format scanner — one of those we use for architectural drawings. that would be perfect because some of the letters are on larger paper, almost A3 size. he wrote long letters. he had a lot to say and a lot to mourn

A large format architectural scanner is ideal — it'll handle A3 sheets without folding or cropping, and the resolution on those machines is usually excellent. Just make sure the glass is clean and the settings are on document mode rather than photo mode for the sharpest text rendering. I'd also recommend saving as TIFF for archival quality alongside JPEGs for everyday use.

TIFF, yes, good thinking — I want to preserve every detail. now, for the actual calligraphy practice, what kind of qalam should I start with? I have seen bamboo pens and reed pens and I do not know which is better for a beginner

For a beginner, bamboo is slightly more forgiving — it's harder and more durable than reed, so it holds its edge longer and is less likely to split while you're still developing pressure control. Start with a nib width of about 8-10mm, cut at roughly a 45-degree angle. Once you're comfortable with bamboo, you can move to reed, which is more traditional and gives a softer, more nuanced line — but it wears faster and requires more pen control.

You can buy pre-cut calligraphy pens from Arabic calligraphy supply shops online — Kalaam or Arabic Calligraphy Services both ship internationally.

bamboo first, then. and do you have any book or video recommendations for Naskh specifically? I do not want general Arabic calligraphy — I want to understand the geometry and proportions of this particular script

For Naskh specifically:

- **"The Art of Arabic Calligraphy" by Mamoun Sakkal** — great on the geometric proportions that underlie Naskh letterforms.
- **Hassan Massoudy's books** — he's an Iraqi-French calligrapher. "Calligraphie Arabe Vivante" is a classic that covers Naskh in depth.
- **YouTube: Joumana Medlej** — excellent breakdowns of Naskh letter anatomy and stroke order.
- **"Naskh Script" workbook by Mustafa Ja'far** — structured exercises specifically for beginners.

The Sakkal book is probably where I'd start since you're interested in the underlying geometry — it'll help you see the mathematical logic behind your grandfather's curves.

wonderful. I will order the Sakkal book and a bamboo qalam this week and scan the letters on Monday at the office. yalla, time to become a student again. it is a strange and good feeling to be a beginner at something after decades of expertise in another discipline. humbling. like standing before a building you do not yet know how to read

There's real value in being a beginner again — it sharpens attention in a way expertise can dull. And you have an advantage most calligraphy students don't: a personal, emotional connection to the specific hand you're learning. That motivation will carry you through the frustrating early stages. Good luck with the scanning and the qalam — I hope his letters open up beautifully.

thank you for this. I suspect that once I start I will not want to stop. the best projects are like that — the ones that feel less like work and more like remembering. like restoration, in a way. bringing something back
That's exactly what it is — restoration of a different kind. Enjoy the process.
